summary: Searched docs/solutions/ for learnings applicable to the 12-unit hidden-debt hardening sweep (webui_store singleton fix, budget TOML additions, dead-noqa cleanup, ~100 except-Exception classification with 3 targeted behavioral fixes, audit reports, benchmarks). Also did light git archaeology on the two named docs' 'related issues' trail and on the exact commits touching the 3 flagged fixes, which surfaced an important UNDOCUMENTED prior incident (an archived branch's near-identical except-Exception classification sweep that regressed on narrowed except-tuples). Full detail below.
critical_patterns_note: docs/solutions/patterns/critical-patterns.md does not exist in this repo; no critical-patterns section to include.

named_docs_read_in_full.content_summary: Audit of silent except-Exception swallows in publishing/adapters/* (linkedin_api.py, medium_browser.py, blogger_api.py, devto_api.py, notion_api.py). Establishes 4 rules: (1) no unlogged except Exception/pass, (2) prefer specific exception types over broad catch-alls, (3) maintain fallback boundaries with a comment + log.debug explaining the optional/best-effort nature, (4) preserve tracebacks with `from exc`. Gives an explicit before/after diff for medium_browser.py's Save Draft click fallback: the blueprint's own prescribed fix was only to ADD A LOG LINE and keep falling through to `page.wait_for_timeout(3000)` -- i.e. log-and-continue, not raise.
named_docs_read_in_full.consistency_check_vs_current_diff: PARTIAL DIVERGENCE, but a well-justified/stronger one. Commit a5f8ba3a (D2) does NOT follow this doc's Save Draft blueprint literally -- instead of log+continue, it now raises ExternalServiceError with an inline comment citing this exact doc ('named a critical silent swallow in docs/solutions/correctness/adapter-silent-exceptions-resolution.md') and explaining that log+continue would produce a false 'drafted' success with no independent recheck step (K8) to catch it. This is MORE conservative than the blueprint, not less -- the diff essentially says 'the documented fix undersells the severity; raising is correct because there's no downstream verification of the draft actually saving.' Flag this only so the reviewer double-checks the raise doesn't break retry/orchestration semantics upstream of the Save Draft try/except (i.e. does the enclosing retry_transient_call / publish loop treat ExternalServiceError from mid-publish the same way it treats other publish failures, or does it need a status='drafted-unconfirmed' partial-success path instead of a hard failure). All other adapter sites in this blueprint (linkedin_api.py, blogger_api.py, devto_api.py/notion_api.py JSON-parse fallbacks) were NOT touched by this diff at all -- the diff's D2 scope stayed inside medium_browser.py plus a debt_registry.toml labeling pass across ~30 other adapter files (see below); it did not revisit the blueprint's Rule-2 'catch ValueError not Exception' recommendation for those untouched sites.

named_docs_read_in_full.content_summary: Read-side projector silently dropped publish successes because an unrecognized status string fell through a silent `else`. Fix: route every status through a 3-outcome classifier (kind | NO_EMIT | QUARANTINE) so unknown-from-authoritative-source input is never dropped, only ever recognized/declared-no-op/quarantined. Also documents a WAL nested-connection deadlock caught mid-implementation (defer secondary writes until after the reducer transaction commits). Prevention section: 'guard both seams, not just output', 'never let an authoritative-source value hit a silent else', 'test presence AND absence per path'.
named_docs_read_in_full.consistency_check_vs_current_diff: Not directly touched by this diff (no events/kinds.py or projector changes in the commit range) but the diff's D3 unit does touch the same events/ package (events/scrubber.py is imported into _driver_impl.py, bind_channel.py, chrome_backend.py; events/_project_helpers.py, projector.py, publish_writer.py, reconcile.py, reconciler.py get 2-7 line changes in c104dd95). The classification discipline this doc argues for (never silently drop/never silently degrade an authoritative signal) is the same spirit as this sweep's move from 'log+continue' to 'raise' in medium_browser.py, and same spirit as NOT letting exception text silently leak into a closed-enum field (chrome_backend.py fix, see below) -- i.e. the current diff is philosophically aligned with this doc even though it doesn't touch the exact files that doc fixed. No contradiction found.

silent_swallow_findings.verified_diff: Confirmed via `git diff` that the Save Draft except-clause now does: `raise ExternalServiceError("Medium Save Draft click failed; draft status is unconfirmed: ...") from exc` instead of the prior log.warning + page.wait_for_timeout(3000) fallthrough. Inline comment explicitly cites the resolution doc above and explains the false-success rationale. This matches the *spirit* of the correctness doc (never silently swallow) and goes further than its literal blueprint (see divergence note above) -- recommend this is intentional and fine, but is exactly the kind of change that should have an accompanying test proving the ExternalServiceError actually propagates as a publish failure (not caught by an outer boilerplate `except Exception` that would re-swallow it one frame up). Note: this same medium_browser.py file HAS an outer `except Exception as exc:` at the bottom of the publish() method (tagged `# debt: medium-browser-publish-boilerplate-wrap-accepted` in this same diff) that calls `_save_screenshot` then does `context.close(); browser.close(); raise` (re-raises, doesn't swallow) -- so the new ExternalServiceError should propagate correctly, but worth the correctness reviewer's explicit confirmation since it's the crux of the fix.

credential_leakage_findings.docs_found_directly_on_topic: No docs/solutions entry specifically documents a PAST credential/token leak via logs or stdout in production code. The closest match, docs/solutions/best-practices/self-doc-sanitization-leak-recurrence-2026-05-15.md, is about a DIFFERENT problem: authors of docs/plans/solution-write-ups repeatedly leaking real operator tokens INTO THE DOCS THEMSELVES while writing about how to sanitize them (a documentation-authoring hazard, not a runtime/logging code hazard). Not directly on-point for bind_channel.py/_driver_impl.py's runtime exception-text scrubbing, but the underlying discipline ('the act of writing/emitting near a secret does not exempt you from the gate') is the same spirit.
credential_leakage_findings.actual_mechanism_used_in_diff: The D3 commit (c104dd95) wires `backlink_publisher.events.scrubber.scrub_text` (an existing, already-implemented module with regex + Shannon-entropy secret detection -- oauth bearer, JWT, google api key, sha256-hex, basic-auth URLs, cookie/authorization headers, session/csrf tokens) into 3 previously-unscrubbed exception-text emission points: chrome_backend.py's ChromeLaunchError wrapping, _driver_impl.py's PersistIOError message (storage_state persistence failures can embed repr()'d cookie/session content), and bind_channel.py's two catch-all stdout->WebUI emit sites (`channel.bind.failed` event's `message` field, which is the actual unfiltered exit seam to the operator's browser).
credential_leakage_findings.assessment: This is REUSE of existing, already-battle-tested infrastructure (scrub_text already has call sites in events/_project_reducers.py per its own docstring) rather than a new ad hoc redaction scheme -- good practice, low risk of a fresh bug class. No past-learning doc was found that this specific wiring contradicts. Recommend the correctness/security reviewer spot-check that `scrub_text` is applied BEFORE the exception text is embedded in any field that a closed-enum consumer reads verbatim (see next finding, which is the adjacent contract this same commit also fixes).

closed_enum_contract_findings.chrome_backend_error_code_fix: Verified via `git diff` on src/backlink_publisher/cli/_bind/chrome_backend.py and _driver_impl.py. Before: `_chrome_profile_dir()` caught `_ChromeSessionError` and did `raise ChromeLaunchError(str(exc)) from exc` -- stuffing arbitrary (unscrubbed) exception text into `error_code`, a field `webui_app/services/bind_job.py` treats as a closed-enum lookup key via `BIND_ERROR_MESSAGES.get(error_code, ...)`. After: `ChromeLaunchError.__init__` gained a separate keyword-only `detail` attribute; the fix now does `raise ChromeLaunchError("chrome_not_available", detail=cleaned) from exc` where `cleaned = scrub_text(str(exc))[0]` -- error_code stays a fixed literal, the free-text (scrubbed) detail goes on a field bind_job.py's enum lookup never reads.
closed_enum_contract_findings.no_past_doc_found_on_this_exact_contract: No docs/solutions entry documents this exact 'don't stuff exception text into a closed-enum field' pattern as a past bug. It reads as a first-time, well-reasoned fix rather than a repeat of a documented mistake. Recommend this pairing (error_code stays enum, detail carries scrubbed free text) itself become a docs/solutions entry post-merge via /ce-compound, since it's a reusable contract other closed-enum fields in this codebase (e.g. any BIND_ERROR_MESSAGES-style dict) could benefit from.

lazystore_findings.governance_doc_summary: webui-config-request-cache-governance-2026-06-03.md documents an unrelated-but-adjacent webui_app governance pattern (per-request config memoization via _g_cache, write-handler exclusion list, CC-30 complexity backstop extended to webui_app/) -- not about webui_store/_LazyStore directly, but same governance-discipline neighborhood (explicit registries + CI gates + rationale strings).
lazystore_findings.publish_history_doc_summary: publish-history-helper-invariant-2026-05-20.md documents a 'shared-emit-helper' invariant pattern in webui_store/webui_app: writes to the history store must route through one canonical helper (_push_history_per_row) so an invariant (status=published => url non-empty) can't be silently bypassed by ad hoc direct store writes. Same prevention family as the dofollow-canary-verdict-dropped doc (route every emit path through one chokepoint).
lazystore_findings.current_diff_A1_fix: Verified via `git diff` on webui_store/__init__.py (commit 1d6e6d01): adds `from .verify_health import verify_health_store` and `from .error_reports import error_report_store` imports, adds both to the `_refresh_paths()` store-reset tuple, and adds both names to `__all__`. This is exactly the gap the repo's own CLAUDE.md already documents in the 'WebUI Layout' section (a live-recomputed '10 _LazyStore stores' count that explicitly calls out verify_health_store as 'previously left out of the count' because it was never re-exported through __init__.py's __all__). This diff's A1 fix directly closes that documented gap -- consistent, no conflict, low-risk, matches the exact narrative CLAUDE.md already recorded.
lazystore_findings.no_negative_findings: No docs/solutions entry describes a PAST incident where a webui_store singleton being left out of __all__/_refresh_paths caused a production bug (e.g. stale test isolation, cross-test state bleed) -- so this is a preventive/hygiene fix, not a fix-of-a-documented-recurrence. Worth flagging for /ce-compound after this lands, since the failure mode (a _LazyStore singleton silently omitted from the reset-path) is exactly the shape of bug the repo's test-isolation docs (docs/solutions/test-failures/del-os-environ-poisons-session-scoped-config-dir-fixture-2026-05-27.md, tests-coupled-to-operator-config-state-2026-05-18.md) warn about generally -- i.e. this diff is proactively closing a hole that class of doc would predict causes trouble.

budget_ceiling_and_debt_registry_governa.existing_docs: docs/solutions/best-practices/webui-config-request-cache-governance-2026-06-03.md and docs/solutions/architecture-health-audit-2026-06-01.md both discuss the two-gate model (monolith_budget.toml SLOC ceilings + complexity_budget.toml CC ceilings) and the '>=80 char rationale required, raised in the same PR' convention -- this diff's B1/B2 units (`0441486f` add missing budget entries for 14 untracked files; `c8c64a38` pre-emptively raise registry.py/spec.py ceilings) are straightforwardly consistent with this already-documented governance pattern. No conflict.
budget_ceiling_and_debt_registry_governa.debt_registry_toml_specifically: No docs/solutions entry exists yet documenting debt_registry.toml's own governance model (the schema comment block inside debt_registry.toml itself is thorough: slug/severity/rationale>=80char/discovered/owner/status/location array, anti-gaming rules requiring unique (slug,location) pairs and no byte-identical rationale strings, a cross-reference test that ties every `# debt: <slug>` code comment back to a registry location entry). This is new-ish infrastructure (first debt_registry.toml entry dated 2026-06-04 per the file's own content) that hasn't yet had a docs/solutions writeup. Given this sweep is the largest consumer of it to date (~102 new debt-tagged sites across D1-D3), recommend flagging to the caller that a debt_registry.toml governance doc is a good /ce-compound candidate once this sweep lands -- there is currently NO documented prior-art to check this diff's usage against, which cuts both ways (nothing to contradict, but also no established convention to lean on beyond the TOML file's own inline schema comments).

IMPORTANT_undocumented_prior_incident_fo.headline: This diff's D1-D3 exception-classification work is structurally the SAME class of task that, on a DIFFERENT (now-archived) branch, produced a systemic regression caught by code review 4 days before this branch's merge-base -- but that regression and its fix were never captured as a docs/solutions entry, so a plain docs/solutions search would never surface it. Found only by noticing the git log --all mentions a 'D2 classification' commit not reachable from this branch and investigating why.

evidence.orphan_commit: 3e3666f9 'fix(seam): widen narrowed except-types missed by D2 classification', dated 2026-07-02, only reachable via `refs/tags/archive/feat-phase3-sprint-b-frontend-stabilization` (an archived tag, not on any active branch, not an ancestor of this branch's merge-base 667e5cce nor of HEAD).
evidence.what_it_fixed: Its own commit message: 'Code review found a systemic pattern across D2's except-Exception narrowing: several narrowed except tuples don't actually cover the failure mode their own debt-registry rationale describes, silently reintroducing crashes the original bare except prevented.' Three concrete instances: (1) net_safety.py::_resolve_host_ips narrowed to `except OSError` but missed `UnicodeError` (getaddrinfo raises this -- a ValueError subclass, not OSError -- on IDNA-invalid hostnames), breaking _check_url_for_ssrf's documented never-raises/SSRF-guard contract; (2) config/tokens.py::_load_token narrowed to `except (json.JSONDecodeError, OSError)` but missed `UnicodeDecodeError` for a non-UTF-8 token file; (3) webui_app/helpers/contexts.py::_render's fallbacks named sqlite3.Error and DependencyError in their own rationale text but didn't actually catch them.
evidence.current_state_on_this_branch: Verified via `grep -n` that on THIS branch's current HEAD, net_safety.py:80 is still exactly `except OSError:` (no UnicodeError) and config/tokens.py:75 is still exactly `except (json.JSONDecodeError, OSError):` (no UnicodeDecodeError) -- i.e. the underlying bugs the archived-branch code review found are PRESENT AND UNFIXED on this branch's main lineage right now. webui_app/helpers/contexts.py currently still has broad `except Exception:` at multiple sites (lines 197/203/209/226/240/245/278/283) rather than the narrowed-but-buggy version the archived branch had -- so that file's specific 3-part bug doesn't apply verbatim here, but it does mean contexts.py is a candidate for the exact same class of mistake WHEN it eventually gets narrowed (e.g. by a future D-unit sweep).
evidence.overlap_with_current_diff: NONE of net_safety.py, config/tokens.py, or webui_app/helpers/contexts.py are touched anywhere in this diff (667e5cce..HEAD) -- confirmed via `git diff --stat` returning empty for all three paths. So this diff does not re-touch the exact broken lines.

why_this_still_matters_for_this_review: 1. Pre-existing latent bug, adjacent to this sweep's exact domain: net_safety.py's SSRF-guard function (_check_url_for_ssrf, documented as 'never raises') and config/tokens.py's token loader both currently have a narrowed except-tuple that misses a real failure mode from the underlying stdlib call, per a code review finding on an archived branch that never made it back to main. This is squarely in-genre for this sweep's stated goal (harden except-Exception hygiene, debt_registry.toml governance) even though the current diff's file list doesn't include them. Worth a follow-up ticket/debt_registry.toml entry, or at minimum a call-out in the E1/E3 audit docs this sweep is producing.

2. Pattern risk for the current diff's own ~102 classified sites: the archived branch's mistake was specifically in cases where it ACTUALLY narrowed an `except Exception` to a specific tuple (not just labeled it). Good news verified by sampling this diff's actual changes (git show a5f8ba3a, c104dd95): the overwhelming majority of this diff's 56+46 'classified' sites are NOT narrowed at all -- they keep `except Exception` and only gain a one-line `# debt: <slug>-accepted` comment plus a debt_registry.toml entry. Only medium_browser.py's Save Draft (raises now, doesn't swallow -- widens/strengthens, doesn't narrow), chrome_backend.py's ChromeLaunchError contract fix, and the scrub_text wiring in _driver_impl.py/bind_channel.py involve actual behavior changes, and none of those three narrow an except-type's matched exception classes -- they change WHAT HAPPENS on an already-broad Exception catch (raise instead of swallow; separate error_code from detail; scrub text before emit). So this diff structurally avoids the archived branch's specific failure mode (narrowing that drops real exception types) for its bulk work. This is a materially safer strategy and is worth stating explicitly as a positive finding, not just an absence of contradiction.

3. Workflow/branch-hygiene angle: this workspace's own memory notes (project_shared_directory_hazard, docs/solutions/workflow-issues/foreign-agent-wip-spreads-across-worktrees-2026-05-20.md, external-agent-edits-in-shared-worktree-2026-05-18.md) describe exactly this kind of hazard -- parallel worktree/branch sessions doing overlapping work that can silently diverge or get lost on archival. The 3e3666f9 fix appears to be exactly that: real, tested, valuable work (5 files, 6 new tests) that got orphaned when its branch was archived and never reconciled into main. Recommend surfacing this to the human operator as a separate action item: either cherry-pick 3e3666f9's 3 fixes into main directly, or at minimum open a debt_registry.toml entry for the two live bugs (net_safety.py UnicodeError, config/tokens.py UnicodeDecodeError) so they don't stay invisible.
IMPORTANT_undocumented_prior_incident_fo.recommended_follow_up: Not a blocker for THIS diff (no touched file overlaps), but strongly recommend the caller/orchestrator surface this as a separate finding to the operator: (a) cherry-pick or reapply commit 3e3666f9's 3 fixes (net_safety.py, config/tokens.py, contexts.py) onto main -- they are small, well-tested, and fix live bugs; (b) consider documenting the general pattern ('narrowed except-tuple must be verified against the actual exception types the wrapped stdlib/library call can raise for the failure mode named in its own rationale -- test the actual failure mode, not just that the narrowed type doesn't break the happy path') as a docs/solutions/best-practices entry via /ce-compound, since this is the second time this exact class of exception-classification work has happened in this repo and the first time's lesson was never captured where a docs/solutions search would find it.

other_related_docs_read_for_context: docs/solutions/best-practices/bind-channel-diagnostic-playbook-2026-05-20.md -- 5 rules for diagnosing Playwright bind failures (channel-status.json as source of truth, sync-not-background repro, SQLite cookie inspection, idle-vs-absolute timeout semantics, clean operator handoff). Not directly implicated by this diff's D3 changes (which are about exception-text hygiene, not bind-diagnosis workflow) but same module family (cli/_bind).

docs/solutions/best-practices/credential-rotation-tests-cover-bootstrap-race-2026-05-19.md -- credential state-mutation races (rotation/bootstrap/migration/rebind/cleanup) must all route through one locking primitive and each get a threading.Barrier test. Not directly touched by this diff (no credential rotation/locking code changed), but relevant background for anyone touching cli/_bind/ credential paths going forward.

docs/solutions/best-practices/medium-httponly-auth-cookies-spike-3a-2026-05-19.md -- cookie taxonomy (sid/rid=auth, cf_clearance/_cfuvid/xsrf=ambient) for Medium's bind sanity gate. Not touched by this diff. Background only.

docs/solutions/architecture-health-audit-2026-06-01.md -- prior audit concluding the codebase is already well-modularized and further 'comprehensive optimization' requests should be checked against this doc first; flags webui_store's cross-layer import as a deliberate, accepted decision (not a defect) and lease_management.py as the one true orphan. Relevant background confirming webui_store's placement (repo root, not src/) is intentional -- so this diff's A1 fix operating on webui_store/__init__.py is operating within the accepted architecture, not against it.

docs/solutions/architecture-patterns/2026-06-05-lite-accepted-deferrals.md -- R7 (G5b cross-process rehydrate), R8 (Pydantic opt-in with schema.py as sole authoritative validator), R10 (recheck timeout), R5 (webui.db same-key lost-update, documented+accepted for single-operator LITE). Directly relevant to this sweep's E3 unit ('verify LITE deferral trigger conditions R7/R8/R10') -- E3's audit should cross-check against the exact resume-trigger conditions this doc specifies (e.g. R7 resumes when an unattended/scheduled recheck is introduced; R8 resumes on a 3rd Pydantic/schema.py drift or any new SSRF/injection-relevant payload field; R10 resumes on unattended recheck or an observed hang). Recommend the E3 audit doc (176865c0) be checked against this doc's exact trigger wording rather than re-deriving triggers from scratch, since this doc is the authoritative source for what 'still deferred' means for R7/R8/R10.
recommendations: Confirm the medium_browser.py Save Draft raise (D2) propagates as a genuine publish failure and isn't re-swallowed by the file's own outer boilerplate except-wrap -- add/verify a test that asserts a Save-Draft-click exception surfaces as adapter failure, not a silently-succeeded 'drafted' status.

No blocking conflicts found between this diff and any docs/solutions entry -- the diff's approach (label-in-debt-registry rather than narrow-the-except-type for the bulk of ~102 sites) is a materially safer strategy than a prior, now-archived attempt at the same class of work, which regressed by narrowing except-tuples that didn't actually cover their own stated failure mode.

Surface the orphaned commit 3e3666f9 (archive/feat-phase3-sprint-b-frontation-stabilization tag) to the operator as a separate, non-blocking follow-up: it fixes 2 live bugs still present on main today (net_safety.py._resolve_host_ips missing UnicodeError; config/tokens.py._load_token missing UnicodeDecodeError) that are squarely in this sweep's own problem domain but outside this diff's touched-file set.

Cross-check the E3 audit doc (LITE deferral triggers R7/R8/R10) against docs/solutions/architecture-patterns/2026-06-05-lite-accepted-deferrals.md's exact resume-trigger wording rather than re-deriving from scratch.

After this sweep lands, good /ce-compound candidates: (a) the error_code-stays-enum / detail-carries-scrubbed-text contract from chrome_backend.py's fix, (b) the debt_registry.toml governance model itself (schema, anti-gaming rules, cross-reference test) -- currently undocumented outside its own inline TOML comments, (c) the general 'verify a narrowed except-tuple against the actual exception types the call can raise for the failure mode its own rationale names, not just against the happy path' lesson from the orphaned 3e3666f9 commit, so it's findable next time instead of requiring git archaeology.
